walkthrough.title: Lay Out the City Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Celestia set up! First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. You will see eight cities arranged in a path from left to right, each one more valuable than the last. The cities go from humble little villages on the left to glittering palaces on the right. This is the route our airship will travel.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Treasure Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the treasure. Separate the treasure cards into piles by city number. Each city has its own stack of treasure cards with different point values. Place each pile face down next to its matching city on the board. The early cities have small treasures, and the later cities have the big point cards everyone is chasing.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Equipment Deck
walkthrough.text: Find all the equipment and power cards. Shuffle them together into one face-down deck. Deal eight cards to each player. This is your starting hand, and it is secret. Place the rest of the deck nearby as a draw pile. Your hand is everything in this game, so guard it well.

walkthrough.title: Board the Airship
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching passenger pawn. Everyone places their pawn on the airship, which starts at the very beginning of the board before City one. Pick a starting Captain by any method you like. The Captain will be the one rolling dice and playing cards. Ready to fly? Let's learn how to play!

summary.title: Lay Out the City Board
bullets: Place game board centrally — 8 cities from left to right
Cities increase in treasure value from 1 → 8

summary.title: Sort the Treasure Cards
bullets: Separate treasure cards by city number
Place each pile face-down next to its matching city
Early cities = small VP, later cities = big VP

summary.title: Prepare the Equipment Deck
bullets: Shuffle equipment + power cards into 1 deck
Deal 8 cards to each player (secret hand)
Remaining cards = draw pile

summary.title: Board the Airship
bullets: Each player takes a colored passenger pawn
All pawns start on the airship before City 1
Choose a starting Captain by any method

walkthrough.title: The Journey Begins
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Celestia works! The game is a series of journeys. Each journey, the airship tries to fly from city to city, collecting increasingly valuable treasure along the way. The Captain rolls dice to see what hazards appear, and passengers decide whether to stay aboard or bail out. First player to reach fifty victory points in treasure wins the game!

walkthrough.title: The Captain Rolls Dice
walkthrough.text: At the start of each leg of the journey, the Captain rolls the dice. Cities one through four require two dice. Cities five and six require three dice. Cities seven and eight require four dice. The dice show hazard symbols: lightning bolts, fog clouds, birds, or blank faces. Blank faces are safe and require nothing. Each lightning, fog, or bird symbol must be countered by the Captain playing the matching equipment card.

walkthrough.title: Passengers Decide: Stay or Disembark
walkthrough.text: Here is the juicy part. After the dice are rolled but before the Captain plays cards, every passenger except the Captain must decide: stay on the airship or disembark at the current city. If you disembark, you hop off, grab the top treasure card from that city, and you are safe for this journey. If you stay, you are betting the Captain can handle the hazards. Go around the table starting with the player to the Captain's left.

walkthrough.title: The Captain Resolves Hazards
walkthrough.text: After all passengers have decided, the Captain must play equipment cards to counter every hazard showing on the dice. Lightning bolts need a Lightning Rod card. Fog clouds need a Foghorn card. Birds need a Compass card. If the Captain can play all the required cards, the ship advances to the next city! If the Captain cannot or chooses not to play the required cards, the ship crashes. Everyone still aboard gets nothing for this journey.

walkthrough.title: Power Cards
walkthrough.text: Besides equipment cards, there are special power cards that shake things up. The Turbo card lets the Captain automatically succeed without playing any equipment cards for that roll. The Jetpack lets a passenger jump off and collect treasure even after the Captain has already failed. The Ejector Seat forces another passenger off the ship. And the Return Flight card lets you go back and grab treasure from a city you already passed. Power cards add chaos and bluffing opportunities.

walkthrough.title: End of a Journey
walkthrough.text: A journey ends in one of two ways. Either the ship crashes because the Captain cannot handle the hazards, or all passengers have disembarked and nobody is left to fly further. When a journey ends, everyone draws back up to eight cards if they have fewer. All passengers re-board the airship at the starting position. The Captain role passes clockwise to the next player. Then a brand new journey begins!

walkthrough.title: Captain Rotation and Bluffing
walkthrough.text: The Captain rotates clockwise after each journey. When you are the Captain, you can bluff! You can act confident even when you are missing cards, hoping passengers bail early so you crash with fewer people affected. Or you can act worried when you actually have great cards, tempting passengers to jump off and leave more treasure for you further down the line. Reading the Captain is half the fun of Celestia.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends as soon as any player reaches fifty or more victory points in collected treasure cards. That player wins! If multiple players cross fifty in the same journey, the player with the highest total wins. Treasure cards are kept face up in front of you so everyone can see how close each player is to winning. Keep an eye on your opponents' scores because the game can end suddenly.

walkthrough.title: Welcome Aboard!
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first flight in Celestia! We are going to walk through a few practice journeys with four example players. Player 1 through Player 4 will take turns being the Captain and making decisions as passengers. One of our players is going to make some classic beginner mistakes, so watch and learn. Let's board the airship!

walkthrough.title: Journey One: Player 1 is Captain
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is our first Captain. The airship is heading toward City one. The Captain rolls two dice and gets one lightning bolt and one blank. That is pretty easy. Only one hazard to deal with. Before the Captain plays any cards, the passengers get to decide: stay or jump off.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Stays for City One
walkthrough.text: City one treasure is tiny, so nobody wants to bail here. Players 2, 3, and 4 all stay on the airship. Smart move! Player 1 plays a Lightning Rod card to counter the lightning bolt, and the ship sails safely to City one. Now the dice get rolled again for the next leg toward City two.

walkthrough.title: Heading to City Two
walkthrough.text: The Captain rolls two dice again for City two. This time it is two fog symbols. That means the Captain needs two Foghorn cards. Player 3 looks at this and thinks the odds are getting worse. But Player 4 is not worried at all. Player 4 figures why grab a tiny City two treasure when there are huge treasures ahead?

walkthrough.title: Player 4's First Mistake: Always Riding
walkthrough.text: Player 3 wisely disembarks at City two, grabs a small treasure card, and is safe. Player 4 stays aboard, dreaming of City seven or eight treasure. The Captain plays two Foghorn cards and the ship advances. But here is the thing: Player 4 did not even look at the Captain's remaining hand size. The Captain just spent three cards in two legs and is running low. Player 4 is flying blind.

walkthrough.title: The Ship Pushes to City Three
walkthrough.text: The Captain rolls for City three: one bird and one lightning. Player 2 takes a look at the Captain's hand. Only five cards left and two hazards to counter. Player 2 decides those are decent odds and stays. Player 4 does not even glance at the Captain's hand. Of course Player 4 stays. The Captain plays a Compass and a Lightning Rod. The ship makes it to City three!

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Makes a Smart Exit
walkthrough.text: Rolling for City four: two lightning bolts and a fog. The Captain only has three cards left. Player 2 counts the hazards, counts the Captain's cards, and decides this is too risky. Player 2 disembarks at City three and grabs a nice mid-value treasure card. Player 4? Still on board. Still chasing the dream.

walkthrough.title: Crash! Player 4 Gets Nothing
walkthrough.text: The Captain looks at their hand. They have a Lightning Rod and a Foghorn, but they need two Lightning Rods and a Foghorn. They cannot handle both lightning bolts. The ship crashes! Player 4 gets absolutely nothing for this entire journey. Meanwhile, Player 2 and Player 3 walked away with treasure. This is the classic Celestia trap: always staying aboard hoping for the jackpot.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Take Profits Along the Way
walkthrough.text: Here is the lesson. You do not need to ride the airship to City eight every time. Small and medium treasures add up fast. A player who consistently grabs City three and four treasures will reach fifty points way before someone who crashes four times chasing City eight. Take profits along the way and live to fly another journey.

walkthrough.title: Journey Two: Player 4 Overreacts
walkthrough.text: New journey! Everyone re-boards and Player 2 is now Captain. The Captain rolls for City one: one bird and one blank. Player 4, still stinging from the crash, panics and immediately disembarks at the very first city. They grab the tiniest treasure card in the game. Everyone else stays, the Captain easily plays a Compass, and the ship sails on to bigger prizes.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Second Mistake: Bailing Too Early
walkthrough.text: The ship makes it all the way to City five with no problems. Players 1 and 3 disembark there and grab great treasure cards. Player 4 is sitting with a measly City one card while everyone else scored big. Going from always staying to always bailing is not the fix. The key is reading the situation each time the dice are rolled.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read the Captain's Hand
walkthrough.text: Here is the real skill in Celestia. Before you decide to stay or go, count the Captain's cards. A Captain with seven cards in hand can probably handle most rolls. A Captain with two cards left is a ticking time bomb. Also notice which cards the Captain has been playing. If they have used three Lightning Rods already, they might not have any left for the next roll.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 Gets It Right
walkthrough.text: Journey three! Player 3 is Captain. The ship has made it to City three smoothly. Rolling for City four: two birds and a fog. That is three hazards with three dice. Player 4 looks at the Captain's hand: six cards remaining. Three hazards out of six cards? Those are reasonable odds. Player 4 stays. The Captain plays three cards and the ship advances. Player 4 disembarks at City four with a solid treasure card. Now that is smart play!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations, Sky Sailors!
walkthrough.text:
Congratulations! You have seen the core of Celestia in action. You watched Player 4 learn the two biggest lessons: do not always ride to the bitter end, and do not always bail at the first sign of trouble. Read the Captain's hand, weigh the risk against the reward, and take your treasure when the odds turn against you. Now go fly for real!

Before you launch into a full game, check out General Tips for more strategy and the Appendix for a quick-reference on cards and city tiers. And remember, your GM Guide is always here. Just tap the Q&A tab if you have questions mid-flight. Have fun up there!

walkthrough.title: Count the Captain's Cards
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Celestia is counting the Captain's hand. Before every stay-or-go decision, glance at how many cards the Captain is holding. A Captain with six or seven cards can probably handle anything. A Captain with two or three cards is living on a prayer. Also track what types of cards they have been playing. If the Captain has burned through three Foghorns already, they probably do not have another one.

walkthrough.title: Small Treasures Add Up
walkthrough.text: New players always want to ride to City seven or eight for the big treasure. But here is the math: three City three or four treasures often equal one City seven treasure, and you are way more likely to actually collect them. Consistent small grabs beat occasional big grabs that come with frequent crashes. Think of it like poker. You do not need to win the biggest pot. You need to win more pots than you lose.

walkthrough.title: When You Are Captain, Bluff
walkthrough.text: Being Captain is your chance to mess with everyone's heads. If you have amazing cards, act worried. Sigh, stare at the dice, shake your head a little. Passengers might bail early, leaving you to fly further and grab bigger treasure for yourself if you are the last one aboard. If your hand is terrible, act confident. Smirk, casually pick up the dice. Passengers might stay on board and crash with you, wasting their journey too.

walkthrough.title: Save Your Power Cards
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to use a Turbo card on an early city roll, but that is usually a waste. Save your power cards for critical moments. A Turbo card is worth the most when the ship is heading to City six or seven and the dice show three hazards. A Jetpack is your insurance policy against a crash when the treasure is too good to leave behind. Do not blow your best cards early.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Score Track
walkthrough.text: Treasure cards are public information, so always keep an eye on who is close to fifty points. If someone is at forty-five, you cannot afford to play it safe anymore. You need to push deeper into the cities to grab enough points to catch up or win. Conversely, if you are in the lead at forty-three, sometimes a safe City two treasure is all you need to close it out.

walkthrough.title: Early Journeys: Build Your Hand
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, you have a full hand of eight cards. Use the first few journeys to feel out the game and grab easy treasure. Do not burn through your best equipment cards trying to reach City six on journey one. Remember, you draw back up to eight cards after each journey, so disembarking early and keeping your good cards for later is a perfectly valid strategy.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Be Afraid to Crash as Captain
walkthrough.text: Sometimes as Captain, the right move is to intentionally fail. If three opponents are still on the airship and you are behind on points, crashing the ship hurts them more than it hurts you. You all get nothing, but you denied them treasure. This is especially useful when someone is about to hit fifty points on a big city grab. A well-timed crash can save the game.

walkthrough.title: Adjust Your Risk by Player Count
walkthrough.text: Celestia plays differently at different player counts. With two or three players, the Captain rotation comes around faster, so you get more chances to be Captain and control the dice. With five or six players, you spend more time as a passenger, so reading the Captain becomes even more critical. In larger games, treasure cards also run out faster, making early cities less reliable in late game.

summary.title: Count the Captain's Cards
bullets: Most important skill: check Captain's hand size every decision
6-7 cards = likely safe, 2-3 cards = high crash risk
Track which equipment types they've already played

summary.title: Small Treasures Add Up
bullets: 3 City-3 treasures often = 1 City-7 treasure
Consistent grabs beat occasional big grabs + frequent crashes
Win more pots, not the biggest pot

summary.title: When You Are Captain, Bluff
bullets: Good hand? Act worried → passengers bail → you fly further alone
Bad hand? Act confident → passengers stay → they crash too
Body language and reactions are fair game

Jetpack = insurance for high-value crash moments

Behind? Push deeper for bigger treasure

walkthrough.title: Hand Management as Captain
walkthrough.text: Advanced Celestia is all about hand management. When you are Captain, think about which cards you are willing to spend and which you want to save for when you are a passenger. If you have two Foghorns and three Lightning Rods, you can afford to spend one of each as Captain while keeping reserves. Sometimes it is better to deliberately fail a roll and crash the ship than to burn your last copy of a card type you will need next journey.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what cards other players are spending as Captain. If Player 2 just used their last two Compass cards, you know they are weak against bird hazards when they Captain again. You can also count the total equipment cards played over multiple journeys to estimate what the deck still has left. The better you track card usage, the sharper your stay-or-go decisions become.

walkthrough.title: Targeted Disembark Timing
walkthrough.text: Do not just ask whether the Captain can handle the dice. Ask yourself: what is the value of the treasure I can grab right now versus the expected value of staying? If City three offers a six-point treasure and City four offers an eight-point treasure, staying is only worth two extra points. But if the crash risk jumped from twenty percent to fifty percent, that two-point upside is not worth the risk of losing everything.

walkthrough.title: Metagaming the Captain Role
walkthrough.text: When you know you will be Captain next journey, plan ahead. Hoard specific card types so you can confidently push further as Captain. If you know you are stacked with Lightning Rods, you can afford to be aggressive when lightning shows up. As a bonus, your confident body language will keep passengers on board, making the journey feel more exciting and forcing tough decisions on everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Ejector Seat Tactics
walkthrough.text: The Ejector Seat is one of the meanest cards in the game, and timing it perfectly is an art. The best time to use it is when a rival is close to fifty points and the ship is approaching a high-value city. Eject them before the ship reaches their target city, and they get nothing. You can also use it to eject someone at a low-value city where the treasure is almost worthless. The psychological impact of the Ejector Seat goes beyond the card itself. Just knowing you might have one makes other players nervous.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: When multiple players are near fifty points, the game shifts dramatically. Safe play goes out the window. You need to calculate exactly how many points you need and which city will get you there. If you need eight more points, maybe City four is enough. If you need fifteen, you have to push to City six or seven. In the endgame, it is all about finding the minimum city that closes out the win rather than always reaching for the maximum.

walkthrough.title: Reading Bluffs
walkthrough.text: Experienced Celestia players develop tells just like poker players. Watch for patterns. Does the Captain always hesitate when they are actually confident? Do they speed up when nervous? Some players bluff the same way every time once you know what to look for. Also watch how long a Captain studies the dice versus their hand. A Captain who barely glances at the dice usually already knows they have the cards. A Captain who stares for a long time might be in trouble.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to the Table
walkthrough.text: Finally, adapt your strategy to the players at your table. If everyone plays cautiously and bails early, you can ride further with less competition for high-city treasure. If everyone plays aggressively and stays late, you should take safer exits because crashes will happen more often. The best Celestia players are chameleons. They read the table dynamic and adjust their risk tolerance to exploit whatever strategy the group is not covering.

summary.title: Hand Management as Captain
bullets: Decide which cards to spend as Captain vs. save for passenger turns
Keep reserves of each equipment type when possible
Sometimes deliberately crash rather than burn your last key card

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: Track equipment cards other Captains have spent
If a Captain burned all Compasses → weak vs. bird hazards
Count total cards played to estimate deck composition

summary.title: Targeted Disembark Timing
bullets: Compare current city treasure vs. next city's expected value
Factor in crash probability — 2 extra VP not worth 50% crash risk
Think in expected value, not just best-case scenarios

summary.title: Metagaming the Captain Role
bullets: Hoard specific card types before your Captain turn
Stacked hand → push aggressively as Captain
Confident body language keeps passengers aboard

summary.title: Ejector Seat Tactics
bullets: Best timing: eject rival near 50 VP before a high-value city
Can also eject someone at a low-value city for minimal gain
Psychological threat: opponents play cautiously knowing you might have one

summary.title: The Endgame Push
bullets: Calculate exact VP needed → find the minimum city that wins
Need 8 VP? City 4 might be enough — don't overreach
Need 15 VP? Must push to City 6-7, accept higher risk

summary.title: Reading Bluffs

summary.title: Adapting to the Table

Aggressive table? Bail earlier — crashes happen more often
Best players are chameleons — exploit the uncovered strategy

entries.definition: Equipment card used to counter lightning bolt hazards on the dice. The Captain must play one Lightning Rod for each lightning bolt showing.
Equipment card used to counter fog cloud hazards on the dice. The Captain must play one Foghorn for each fog symbol showing.
Equipment card used to counter bird hazards on the dice. The Captain must play one Compass for each bird symbol showing.
Power card that allows the Captain to automatically succeed on the current dice roll without playing any equipment cards. Best saved for difficult rolls at high-value cities.
Power card that allows a passenger to collect treasure from the current city even after the Captain has failed and the ship crashes. Acts as personal crash insurance.

Power card that forces another passenger off the airship at the current city. The ejected player collects that city's treasure but cannot continue the journey. Can be used strategically to deny rivals access to high-value cities.
Power card that allows a player to go back and collect a treasure card from a city the airship has already passed. Useful for grabbing missed opportunities without leaving the current journey.
The first four cities on the board. The Captain rolls two dice for each leg in this tier. Treasure values are low but the hazards are manageable. Good for consistent small gains.
The middle cities on the board. The Captain rolls three dice for each leg in this tier, increasing the number of potential hazards. Treasure values are moderate to high.
The final two cities on the board. The Captain rolls four dice, making hazards very likely. Treasure values are the highest in the game but reaching these cities is extremely risky.
A hazard symbol on the dice. Each lightning bolt showing must be countered by the Captain playing one Lightning Rod card. If not countered, the ship crashes.
A hazard symbol on the dice. Each fog cloud showing must be countered by the Captain playing one Foghorn card. If not countered, the ship crashes.
A hazard symbol on the dice. Each bird symbol showing must be countered by the Captain playing one Compass card. If not countered, the ship crashes.
A safe result on the dice showing no hazard. Blank faces require no equipment card to counter. The more blanks rolled, the easier the Captain's job.
The player who rolls the dice and must play equipment cards to counter hazards. The Captain role rotates clockwise after each journey. The Captain cannot disembark during their own captaincy.
Any player who is not the current Captain. Passengers ride the airship and must decide each leg whether to stay aboard or disembark to collect treasure.

The act of leaving the airship at the current city to collect a treasure card. Passengers may disembark after the dice are rolled but before the Captain plays equipment cards. Disembarking is permanent for that journey.
When the Captain cannot or chooses not to play the required equipment cards, the airship crashes. All passengers still aboard receive no treasure for that journey. A new journey begins immediately.

One complete trip of the airship from its starting position through the cities. A journey ends when the ship crashes or all passengers have disembarked. After each journey, players draw back to eight cards and re-board.

Victory point cards collected when a passenger disembarks at a city. Each city has its own stack of treasure cards with different VP values. Treasure is kept face up and is public information. First to 50 VP wins.
